era_context: The late 9th century CE continued to be characterized by the fragmentation of the Carolingian Empire, Viking expansion, the decline of the Tang Dynasty in China (Huang Chao Rebellion ongoing), and the consolidation of the Byzantine Macedonian Renaissance. In the Islamic world, the Abbasid Caliphate was fragmenting into regional dynasties. This was a period of significant political instability across Eurasia, with military conflicts reshaping political boundaries.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia (limited sources beyond Islamic chronicles)

events.title: Huang Chao Rebellion: Continued Devastation in Northern China
events.description: The Huang Chao Rebellion continued its destructive campaign across northern China in 878, with rebel forces consolidating control over vast territories and continuing to devastate the countryside. The rebellion had evolved from a localized bandit uprising into a full-scale civil war that was systematically destroying the economic and administrative infrastructure of the Tang Dynasty. Famine and displacement were widespread, with contemporary sources reporting millions of casualties. The rebellion would not be fully suppressed until 884, but by 878 the Tang Dynasty's recovery was already impossible.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian by Sima Guang, compiled 11th century

events.certainty_note: The continuation of the Huang Chao Rebellion in 878 is well-attested in Chinese historical sources, though casualty figures are likely exaggerated.

events.title: Treaty of Meersen: Frankish Empire Division
events.region: Frankish Empire (modern France, Germany, Low Countries)
events.description: In 878, the Treaty of Meersen (also called the Treaty of Meerssen) was negotiated between the sons of Louis the German, further fragmenting the already-divided Carolingian Empire. This treaty formalized the division of the Middle Kingdom (Lotharingia) between Charles the Fat and Louis the Younger, continuing the process of imperial fragmentation that had begun with the Treaty of Verdun (843). These successive divisions weakened central authority and made the Frankish Empire increasingly vulnerable to external threats, particularly Viking raids.

sources.name: Regino of Prüm, Chronicon, compiled early 10th century

events.certainty_note: The Treaty of Meersen in 878 is well-documented in Frankish annals and is a confirmed historical event.

events.title: Viking Siege of Paris (Continuation)
events.description: Viking forces under Ragnar Lothbrok's sons (according to legend) or other Norse leaders (historically) continued to besiege and raid Paris and the Seine valley in 878. The Vikings had established semi-permanent bases in the Seine estuary and were conducting sustained campaigns against Frankish settlements. These raids prompted the construction of fortifications and the payment of tribute (Danegeld) by Frankish rulers. The siege of Paris was part of a broader pattern of Viking pressure on Western Europe during the 870s–880s.
key_figures: Unknown Viking leaders (possibly including sons of Ragnar Lothbrok, though this is legendary)

sources.name: Regino of Prüm, Chronicon, compiled early 10th century
Gesta Normannorum (later Norse sagas), compiled 12th–13th century

events.certainty_note: Viking activity on the Seine in 878 is documented in Frankish annals, but the specific details and dating of individual raids are sometimes uncertain. The legendary attribution to Ragnar Lothbrok's sons is not historically reliable.
cross_references: Part of broader Viking Age expansion
Related to 879-002 (Viking raids on Rhine delta)

disconfirming_evidence: The Treaty of Meersen's exact date has been debated by modern scholars; some sources place it in 880 rather than 878. The legendary accounts of Viking sieges of Paris often conflate multiple raids across different years, making precise dating difficult. The attribution of Viking raids to specific leaders (e.g., Ragnar Lothbrok's sons) is largely legendary and not supported by contemporary sources.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 878 is again heavily weighted toward East Asia (China) and Western Europe (Frankish Empire). The sources are primarily monastic chronicles and later compilations, which tend to emphasize political and military events while neglecting economic and social history. The Byzantine Empire, Islamic world, and regions outside Europe and East Asia are poorly documented for this specific year. Bias toward court-sponsored narratives means that the perspectives of common people, merchants, and non-elite groups are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: The continuation of the Huang Chao Rebellion in 878 is part of the broader rebellion that lasted from 875–884
The Treaty of Meersen in 878 was a consequence of the fragmentation process that began with the Treaty of Verdun in 843
Viking raids on the Seine occurred simultaneously with the Frankish political divisions formalized in the Treaty of Meersen
